section.name: journald-patterns
section.summary: journalctl query patterns for solo-developer VPS service log inspection and incident investigation.

rule.statement: Use --user flag for systemd user services; omit it only for system units like nginx or docker.
rule.rationale: User services run under loginctl linger sessions and their logs are scoped separately. Using system-level journalctl silently returns no output for user services.

rule.statement: Prefer -p err over grepping for "ERROR" strings; journald priority filtering is structured and faster.
rule.rationale: Application logs may write "error" as a string without setting priority. -p err captures systemd-level failures like OOM kills and service crashes regardless of application log format.

example.code:
# Follow a user service in real-time journalctl --user -u nero-core -f
# Last 50 lines of a user service journalctl --user -u nero-core -n 50
# Errors from last 2 hours across all nero services journalctl --user -u 'nero-*' -p err --since "2 hours ago" --no-pager
# Kernel OOM kills journalctl -k --since "1 hour ago" | grep -i "out of memory"
# Service restart events journalctl --user -u nero-core | grep -E "(Started|Stopped|Failed)"
# JSON output for scripted analysis journalctl --user -u nero-core -o json --since "1 hour ago"
# Count errors per service in last 24h for svc in nero-core nero-channel-web nero-channel-tg; do count=$(journalctl --user -u "$svc" -p err --since "24 hours ago" --no-pager 2>/dev/null | wc -l) echo "$svc: $count errors" done

# Wrong: omits --user, returns nothing for user services journalctl -u nero-core -f
# Wrong: grep on raw text is fragile and slow journalctl --user -u nero-core | grep -i error
# Wrong: no --no-pager in scripts causes blocking journalctl --user -u nero-core | wc -l
example.note: Always add --no-pager in scripts. Use -p for priority filtering, not grep on message text.

list.item: -u — filter by unit name; supports glob patterns like 'nero-*'
--user — scope to user-session units (loginctl linger must be enabled)
-p err — priority filter: emerg, alert, crit, err, warning, notice, info, debug
--since / --until — time window; accepts "2 hours ago", "today", ISO timestamps
-o format — output format: short, short-iso, json, cat (message only)
--no-pager — never paginate; required in all scripts

section.name: journald-configuration
section.summary: journald.conf settings to limit disk consumption and ensure logs survive reboots.

rule.statement: Set Storage=persistent so logs survive reboots; default volatile storage loses all history on each boot.
rule.rationale: Incident investigation almost always needs logs from the previous boot cycle. Volatile storage makes post-reboot debugging impossible.

rule.statement: Cap SystemMaxUse at 10% of total disk; for a 100G VPS, 2G is a reasonable ceiling.
rule.rationale: Without a cap, a single chatty service can fill the disk. journald enforces the cap automatically, rotating oldest logs first.

section.reference: journald.conf with size caps, persistence, compression, rate limiting

section.name: docker-log-rotation
section.summary: Configure Docker daemon to rotate container logs before they consume unbounded disk space.

rule.statement: Always configure Docker daemon.json with max-size and max-file before starting any long-running containers.
rule.rationale: Docker's default log driver (json-file) has no rotation. A single high-volume container can fill the disk silently.

example.note: Place at /etc/docker/daemon.json. Restart Docker after changes: sudo systemctl restart docker. Existing containers use old settings until recreated.

antipattern.description: Starting containers without log rotation configured.
antipattern.reason: A container logging 1MB/minute fills a 100G disk in 70 days. With no rotation, the only fix is manual cleanup under incident pressure.

section.name: logrotate
section.summary: logrotate configuration for application log files written outside journald.

rule.statement: Use delaycompress with compress so the currently active log file is never compressed while it may still be written to.
rule.rationale: Compressing an open file corrupts it. delaycompress ensures only the previous rotation's file gets compressed.

section.reference: logrotate config for /var/log/nero-*.log with daily rotation, 14-day retention
